items.title: MNIST digit classifier layout
items.scenario: You flatten each 28x28 grayscale image into a 784-dimensional vector. The network is: input (784) → hidden (128, ReLU) → hidden (64, ReLU) → output (10, softmax logits).
items.explanation: Width of the first hidden layer is 128 neurons; depth is 2 hidden layers. Each of the 128 neurons in layer 1 receives all 784 input features (fully connected). The output layer has 10 neurons — one score per digit class.

items.title: Tabular fraud detection with a shallow net
items.scenario: A bank feeds 40 numeric transaction features into a network: input (40) → hidden (32) → output (1, sigmoid probability of fraud).
items.explanation: Depth 1 (one hidden layer), width 32 in the hidden tier. The single output neuron emits a fraud probability. This is a minimal MLP shape before trying deeper stacks.

items.title: Reading a PyTorch printout of layer sizes
items.scenario: Training logs show `Linear(in_features=784, out_features=256)` followed by `Linear(in_features=256, out_features=10)`.
items.explanation: `in_features` is the width of the previous layer's activations; `out_features` is this layer's width (neuron count). The first linear maps 784 inputs to a 256-wide hidden layer; the second maps 256 hidden activations to 10 output logits.

items.title: Why batching uses matrix multiply
items.scenario: A mini-batch of 64 images is stacked as a matrix X of shape 64 x 784. One weight matrix W of shape 784 x 256 produces hidden activations H = X @ W + b with shape 64 x 256 in one library call.
items.explanation: Each row of X is one example; each column of H is one neuron's activations across the batch. Layers are implemented as matrix operations over batches, not 64 separate forward passes in a Python loop.
